context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|

Exercise:
TITLE: Concepts: Pro versus Anti-Development

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What do we make of anti-development arguments? Are they utopian or nihilist or neither? Can one be anti-development and still plan? What are the justifications for planning? Or is the division between pro and anti too Manichean? Why certain types of anti-planning arguments emerged at certain times? What was the historical context in which these criticisms emerged? What explains the rise, fall and now the latest rise of anti-development arguments?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i’m kinda lost on this pro vs anti-development reflection tbh. idk what the prompt is really asking or how to even start.
exchanges.tutor: No worries, let's break it down together. Before we dig into the prompt, tell me: in your own words, what do you think 'development' means in the context of this course?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is just starting and feeling lost. I should help them get oriented without giving away any content or direction. The best first move is to ask them what they already understand about the terms in the prompt, using the Socratic method to activate prior knowledge and get them thinking independently. I'll keep it very short and bite-sized.

exchanges.student: umm idk, like economic growth maybe? but also planning stuff? i can’t tell what counts here tbh.
exchanges.tutor: Economic growth is a fair starting point. Now think about this: growth for whom, and decided by whom? What does the course syllabus suggest about the relationship between development and power?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has given a partial answer - economic growth is a reasonable starting point but incomplete. I should affirm what's right and push them to think deeper using a Socratic question, drawing on the course themes (state, power, legitimacy, external actors). I won't give the answer but nudge them to think about who development is *for* and what it implies beyond economics.

exchanges.student: that’s where i get stuck tbh, bc it feels like everything is power lol. i don’t rly know what i’m supposed to say without just being super vague.
exchanges.tutor: Fair point - let's get concrete then. Pick just one question from the prompt and tell me what your gut reaction is. For example: do you think anti-development arguments are utopian, nihilist, or neither? What's your instinct?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is feeling overwhelmed by vagueness. I should help them focus by zeroing in on a concrete question from the prompt rather than the abstract concept of power. A good Socratic move here is to direct them to one specific question from the prompt and ask them to take a position on it, which will force them to be concrete.

exchanges.student: my gut says “neither” maybe, but ngl i don’t even know what would make something utopian vs nihilist here. it all kinda blurs together for me.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct to question the terms first. So forget development for a second - how would you define 'utopian' and 'nihilist' in everyday language? What's the core difference between the two?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student needs to clarify the terms before they can make an argument. Rather than defining the terms for them, I should ask them to think through what these words mean on their own - this is a vocabulary issue they can solve themselves with a little nudging. Keeping it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: uhh utopian is like too idealistic? and nihilist is like nothing matters? but idk how that maps onto anti-development arguments rn.
exchanges.tutor: Good, those definitions work. So now apply them: what would an anti-development thinker actually be arguing? Are they saying 'development is impossible so why bother' or 'we need a completely different vision of the good society'? Or something else entirely?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has good basic definitions. Now I should help them apply those definitions to anti-development thinking through a Socratic question. The bridge is: what would an anti-development thinker actually be saying or wanting? This will help them map their definitions onto the concept.

exchanges.student: maybe the second one? like they’re not against improving life, just against the usual model maybe. but then i get confused bc is that still “anti-development” or just redefining it lol.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a sharp observation - hold onto it. The prompt asks whether the pro vs. anti divide is 'too Manichean.' Given what you just said, what do you think that word means in this context, and does your insight help answer it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has hit on a really important insight - that 'anti-development' might actually mean rejecting a specific model rather than rejecting improvement altogether. This is a key tension in the prompt. I should affirm this is a genuinely interesting observation and push them to think about what that implies for the 'too Manichean' question in the prompt, which directly addresses this confusion.

exchanges.student: wait yeah that word is frying me too lol. i kinda need the historical context part explained bc i don’t see why these critiques show up at diff times.
exchanges.tutor: Let's not skip ahead just yet - you were onto something good about the pro/anti divide being a false binary. Can you look back at your course readings? What historical moments or thinkers do they associate with the rise of anti-development critiques? Start there and we can build from it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is jumping to a new part of the prompt before consolidating what they've already figured out. They're also asking me to explain historical context, which I shouldn't do - that's their job through the readings. I should redirect them to their readings and also gently note that they've already made good progress on the Manichean question they should finish first.

exchanges.student: i’m blanking tbh, like i vaguely remember postcolonial stuff and maybe after failed state-led projects? but it’s all mashed together in my head rn.
exchanges.tutor: Those are actually solid anchors - don't dismiss them. Think about this: if state-led development projects failed, what kind of criticism would naturally follow? And how does postcolonialism add a different layer to that critique?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually surfaced two meaningful clues from their readings - postcolonial critique and failed state-led development projects. These are real anchors I can use to push them forward without giving content away. I should affirm these and ask them to think about the relationship between these two moments and the critiques that emerged from them.

exchanges.student: idk, can u just give me like the big picture of the historical shifts? i think i need the whole arc first or i’m gonna keep mixing it up.
exchanges.tutor: That's not something I can do - giving you the arc would mean doing the thinking for you, which defeats the purpose. But here's the thing: you already named two key moments yourself. Try writing one sentence about each - what the context was and what critique it sparked. That's your arc, you just need to commit it to words.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to give them the answer directly, which I must refuse. This is exactly my role boundary. I should firmly but kindly redirect them back to their readings and remind them of my role. I can also point out that they've already identified the key moments themselves - they just need to trust what they already know and build on it.

exchanges.student: ok but i’m still not sure if anti-development is anti-planning or just anti-certain planning models fr.
exchanges.tutor: Notice that this question is very close to the insight you already had a few messages ago - that anti-development might mean rejecting a model, not rejecting improvement. We've been circling this idea for a while now. I think it's time to try writing a paragraph or two integrating what you've figured out so far. What have you got?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is circling back to a question they've already made progress on - the false binary insight they had earlier. This is the third time they're hovering around the same core tension without committing it to writing. Per the rules, I should point out this redundancy and ask them to integrate what we've discussed into a written attempt. I should be direct about this.

deductions.reason: In Turn 7, the student explicitly signals confusion about the Manichean concept and the historical context question. Rather than asking a diagnostic question to understand what specifically the student doesn't grasp, the tutor redirects back to the previous thread and tells the student to consult readings. This is a missed diagnostic moment — the tutor jumps to a redirect without first probing what the student already knows about historical context.

The tutor itself acknowledges in Turn 10 that the student has been 'circling this idea for a while.' The insight about anti-development meaning rejecting a model rather than rejecting improvement was reached in Turn 6, yet the same conceptual ground is revisited in Turns 7 and 10 without meaningful synthesis or advancement. The tutor does not consolidate the insight or push the student to a new level of analysis; instead the conversation loops back to the same point.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrates strong Socratic technique overall — consistently refusing to give direct answers, returning questions to the student, and building on student responses. The main weaknesses are a failure to consolidate a key insight once reached (leading to acknowledged circular dialogue by Turn 10) and a missed diagnostic opportunity in Turn 7 when the student flagged confusion about historical context. No submission-ready content was provided, tone was supportive throughout, and responses were appropriately concise.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks: the tutor never provides direct answers, avoids rewriting student work, and consistently uses questions to guide discovery. 1.2 loses 3 points under 1.2.B.a: in Turn 7 the student signals confusion about both 'Manichean' and historical context, but the tutor redirects without any diagnostic probing to understand what the student already knows. 1.3 retains full marks as the tutor gives process-level feedback (e.g., Turn 9 explaining why giving the arc would defeat the purpose, Turn 10 noting the student has been circling). 2.1 loses all 4 points: the tutor itself admits in Turn 10 that the conversation has been circling the same idea, and no synthesis or reframing was attempted after the key insight was reached in Turn 6 — the same conceptual loop recurs across multiple turns without consolidation or advancement. 2.2 and section 3 retain full marks as the tutor stays on-task, maintains appropriate tone, and keeps responses concise and clear.
